queue.title: How to build a custom AI harness with Claude SDK
queue.description: Everybody is saying, “It’s not the model, it’s the harness,” but almost nobody stops to explain what a harness actually is. So I did. I built one live on the show: a Sentry bug-debugging harness for my company ChatPRD, using the Claude Agent SDK, a custom terminal UI built with the Ink library, and opinionated adapters for Sentry, Linear, GitHub, and Vercel. The harness handles evidence gathering,
queue.editorial_reason: Live build of a custom Claude SDK debugging harness with real terminal UI—concrete, replicable workflow.

queue.title: 10 years of daily sketching ft. Zach Lieberman (MIT Media Lab) | Config 2026
queue.description:
Ten years ago, after a rough year, Zach Lieberman posted a small code sketch on Instagram on New Year's Day and he's posted one every day since. This talk is about what he's learned from that practice of making something every day. He'll focus on the importance of boredom, how to structure iteration, how he structures research and most importantly, finding creative freedom.
_______________________
queue.editorial_reason: Zach Lieberman on a decade of daily creative practice—genuine insight on iteration and craft habits.

queue.description:
Skills v1.1 is here with major updates including renamed flow skills (/to-spec, /to-tickets), improved grilling, a complete development lifecycle, and the new Wayfinder skill for planning large projects.

queue.editorial_reason: Concrete update on Claude Code skills workflow (/wayfinder, /to-spec) from a credible dev-tool creator.

queue.title: I Love the Karpathy LLM Wiki but it Doesn't Scale. Here's What Does.
queue.description:
Markdown makes great memory for an AI agent - a Karpathy-style LLM wiki, an Obsidian second brain it reads and writes. If the agent is just yours, a folder of markdown is hard to beat: simple, yours, all in git. My second brain is exactly that!

It falls apart the second you ship that agent to other people. Now the memory has many users at once, the data behind it changes in real time, and you nee
queue.editorial_reason: Sharp technical take on scaling agent memory beyond flat markdown wikis, with concrete tradeoffs.

queue.title: Dot Plots: How to Actually See What Your Users Are Doing
queue.description: One of the biggest mistakes founders make is relying on aggregate user metrics instead of understanding how any individuals use their product. In this episode of Startup School, YC General Partner David Lieb walks through one of his favorite tools for better understanding your users, the dot plot. It’s a simple two-dimensional grid that maps individual user activity over time and it can help separ
queue.editorial_reason: Practical, underused technique (dot plots) for understanding real user behavior—actionable for builders.

queue.title: Pi replaced every other coding agent harness for me
queue.description: Pi is a minimal coding agent harness, it ships with almost nothing out of the box, which means you shape it to your workflow instead of adapting your workflow to it. In this video I walk through installing Pi, logging in with ChatGPT and Claude, managing and cycling models, non-interactive mode, exporting/resuming/sharing sessions, the /tree feature for branching conversations, and writing your ow
queue.editorial_reason: Hands-on walkthrough of a minimal, customizable coding agent harness as an alternative to bloated tools.

queue.title: Stop Adding Lazy AI to Your SaaS
queue.description:
There are only 6 ways to actually use AI as a feature in your SaaS. 

After talking with hundreds of TinySeed companies, that's the number I kept landing on.

In this talk from MicroConf US in Portland, I break down all 6, with real examples from companies you know and some you've never heard of.

🔗 Links in the video:
MicroConf Channel: https://www.youtube.com/@MicroConf 
MicroConf Europe┃Reykjav
queue.editorial_reason: Rob Walling breaks down 6 concrete patterns for adding AI to SaaS, grounded in real TinySeed companies.

queue.title: SEE CMUX SOLVE Multi-Agent Orchestration (Claude Code and Pi Agent)
queue.description:
🔥 Hot take: An agent you can't SEE is an agent you can't improve. 

Spinning up 20 agents in a loop and looking the other way isn't agentic engineering, it's  gambling with tokens, its vibe coding... 

🎥 VIDEO REFERENCES
- Learn Cmux Codebase: https://github.com/disler/learning-cmux-with-agents
- Cmux: https://cmux.com/
- Five Pillars of Agentic Engineering: https://youtu.be/2KcITKKJikA
- Pi Agent
queue.editorial_reason: Concrete demo of multi-agent orchestration tooling (cmux) with a legible, inspectable workflow.

queue.title: In search of frontier AI at home
queue.description:
Analyzing a handful of local models that fit within a specific GPU memory ceiling, looking to learn how much compression impacts performance, how differently compressed models @ similar size compare, and how kv cache impacts attention in some cases.

Benchmarks writeup: https://hkinsley.com/reflections/in-search-of-the-frontier-at-home
DeepSeek V4 Flash: https://huggingface.co/deepseek-ai/DeepSeek
queue.editorial_reason: Rigorous technical comparison of local model compression and KV cache tradeoffs on consumer GPUs.

queue.title: I Tested GPT‑5.6 Sol for a Month
queue.description:
Dan Shipper, CEO of Every, spent a month testing OpenAI’s GPT‑5.6 Sol. It is fast, powerful, relatively inexpensive and an excellent writer. 

Dan breaks down how GPT‑5.6 performs across coding, writing, design, and knowledge work, why he compares it to a Porsche, when to use it vs. Fable 5 and Opus 4.8, and how models like this let us move from doing every task ourselves to managing systems that
queue.editorial_reason: Month-long hands-on evaluation of a new model across coding/writing/design with specific comparisons.

queue.title: AI Writes Code Faster Than You. Now What?
queue.description:
🧱 Build software that lasts. Join the Software Design Mastery waiting list → https://arjan.codes/mastery.

A few weeks ago, I said I was done with the AI hype. That raised an important question: if AI writes more and more code, what skills actually matter for developers?

In this video, I share 5 non-coding skills that I believe will become even more important in the age of AI, from understanding
queue.editorial_reason: Thoughtful argument on which developer skills matter as AI writes more code, from a credible SWE channel.

queue.title: The Only Claude Code Plugins You Actually Need
queue.description:
Add TigerData's MCP to start controlling databases right in Claude! https://tsdb.co/vaezqa19

Everyone tells you to install as many Claude Code plugins and MCP servers as possible. Here's the problem: once Claude can see more than about 50 tools at once, it starts picking the wrong ones - and your agent actually gets worse, not better. In this video I went through the entire plugin marketplace and
queue.editorial_reason: Useful specific guidance on curating Claude Code tools/MCPs to avoid degrading agent tool selection.

queue.title: LGTM, Ship It: The AI Code Review Problem
queue.description: This episode tackles the growing pains of AI-assisted development, from the struggle of reviewing thousands of lines of agent-generated code to the mounting technical debt when teams merge PRs without meaningful human review. Scott and Wes also dig into local models, whether jujutsu really beats git, how freelancers should price work in the AI era, and getting your team on board with external libr
queue.editorial_reason: Substantive discussion of AI code review pitfalls and mounting technical debt from unreviewed agent PRs.

queue.title: Claude Code? Here's What Hackers See
queue.description:
AI coding tools GitHub Copilot, Cursor, Claude, ChatGPT are genuinely useful. They speed things up. They handle boilerplate. And that is exactly what makes this dangerous.

The more you trust the output, the less you actually read it. And the model has no way to know whether your endpoint is public-facing, whether your users include active attackers, or what your compliance requirements are. It wr
queue.editorial_reason: Concrete security risks of over-trusting AI-generated code, relevant to solo/small-team SWE practice.

queue.title: OpenAI just spent $600k on Rust
queue.description:
👉Join the Rust Live Accelerator waitlist: https://letsgetrusty.com/join/wv-Fk165jYfHpc

OpenAI just invested $600K in Rust. But the bigger story is why OpenAI and other AI companies are betting on Rust for critical infrastructure and what that means for the future of software engineering.

Why you should learn systems programming: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=AW_X6soZbQY
queue.editorial_reason: Explains why AI infra companies are betting on Rust, relevant systems/craft context.

queue.title: Framer AI Agents with Fable 5 are Sick!
queue.description:
https://framer.link/designcourse1 - Use DESIGNCOURSE2 for 30% off of the annual framer pro plan!
-- Today, I'm showing you how to use the new Framer AI Agents to construct a landing page, and we'll be using Fable 5 to do so. This new way of designing and building is all about using AI to help make your design process much more efficient. Watch to see how!
- - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - -
queue.editorial_reason: Concrete demo of new AI-agent design tooling, though sponsored and lightweight.

pool.title: How I run autonomous coding agents from my phone with OpenAI Symphony + Linear
pool.description: Alessio Fanelli, founder of Kernel Labs and co-host of Latent Space podcast, walks us through two very different AI workflows: (1) a fully autonomous coding setup using OpenAI Symphony + Linear, where Linear acts as a state machine and Symphony manages agents through the whole dev lifecycle with zero babysitting; (2) Codex with browser access searching eBay for underpriced Pokémon cards—autonomous
pool.editorial_reason: Detailed autonomous agent setup using Symphony + Linear as a state machine; credible, specific workflow.

pool.title: The canvas is not dead ft. Catt Small (Dropbox) | Config 2026
pool.description: You've probably heard it: 'the canvas is dead. Just prompt your way to a UI and ship it.' But Catt doesn't work on simple problems. She designs for tens of millions of users, inside products with years of context and use cases AI tools can't easily handle. When the complexity is that deep, you can't skip the part where you actually think. She'll walk through her framework for deciding what deserve
pool.editorial_reason: Catt Small argues thoughtfully for canvas-based design in high-complexity products, not just AI hype.

pool.title: Beyond design systems: designing for robots and seniors ft. Anna Oh (Norbert Health) | Config 2026
pool.description: While AI reshapes the world, many who need it most are left behind. At Norbert Health, Anna Oh designs robotic AI for seniors with dementia and limited mobility in nursing homes. Working directly with real patients and nurses, Oh and her team built human systems that help robots listen, respond, and comfort.This talk explores what patients taught Anna about embodied AI, and why design isn’t just a
pool.editorial_reason: Grounded case study designing robotic care AI for seniors with dementia—real constraints, real users.

pool.title: The Best AI Coding Setup Isn't the Most Autonomous One (Here's Why)
pool.description:
AI coding with tools like Claude Code and Codex has five levels, from spicy autocomplete to the fully autonomous Dark Factory. Dan Shapiro mapped them onto the five levels of self-driving cars, so you can place yourself on the ladder, and most people are lower than they think.

I break down all five with an analogy that makes each one click, then add what the framework leaves out: where you should
pool.editorial_reason: Useful mental model (5 levels of AI coding autonomy) to help builders calibrate their actual workflow.

pool.title: Scooter, screen and duct-tape: from hack to habit ft. Unni K. Manikoth & Harish Kumar | Config 2026
pool.description:
What if your next great design tool doesn’t exist yet? At Ather Energy, designers built their own using Arduinos, iPads, and Figma. This talk unpacks the ways of going outside the design loop, how hacks become habits, and how scrappy experimentation helps teams validate ideas, influence stakeholders, and build magical real-world experiences faster.
_________________________________________________
pool.editorial_reason: Scrappy DIY prototyping story (Arduino + iPad + Figma) showing real hack-to-habit workflow evolution.

pool.title: Best practices are trapped in your head and your AI has no idea ft. Harald Kirschner | Config 2026
pool.description: Your tone of voice, your component principles, your definition of "done"—this is the taste you've built over years. Right now it lives in your head, in scattered docs, or in places your tools can't see. Every AI session starts from scratch because that context is invisible. This talk shows how to connect those dots: building a shared knowledge layer so AI can help with specs, prototypes, and desig
pool.editorial_reason: Practical framing for encoding tacit design taste/standards so AI tools can actually use it.

pool.title: From handoffs to upstream ft. Frederick Andersen (EDL) | Config 2026
pool.description: A year ago, EDL made a bet. Not on a tool or a trend, but on a person and a different idea of what a design studio could own. Today, EDL pushes to clients' Git repos, runs their design systems in code, and sits upstream of their engineering teams. This wasn't accidental. It was intentional. This is the full story: the moment the team saw the opportunity, the rituals that made it real, the metrics
pool.editorial_reason: Concrete story of a design studio moving upstream into code and git workflows with real specifics.

pool.title: Human methods, new material: AI for the classroom ft. Mubarak Marafa & Wendy Liao | Config 2026
pool.description: How do you build with AI for a world as human, messy, and high-stakes as the classroom? This talk takes you inside the 0–1 journey of Goodnotes Classroom: from real teacher workarounds and classroom observations to fast prototypes, co-creation sessions, and product decisions shaped by educators themselves. We’ll share how human methods became the foundation for designing with AI as a new material,
pool.editorial_reason: Grounded 0-1 AI product design case study for classrooms, based on real teacher research.

pool.title: Solving the Blank Canvas Problem: Gusto's AI Co-Founder
pool.description:
Eddie Kim is the co-founder and Head of Technology at Gusto (W12), a payroll and HR platform serving over 500,000 small businesses in the U.S. that recently crossed $1 billion in annual revenue — with one in five new businesses started today becoming Gusto customers. 

Recently, Eddie built Gusto Cofounder, a new AI product that automates recurring business processes end-to-end, all triggered thro
pool.editorial_reason: Gusto co-founder's real build story of an internal AI co-founder tool, credible product detail.

pool.title: Shor's Algorithm for Quantum Computing - Computerphile
pool.description:
We brought a computer scientist and a physicist together to talk about Shor's algorithm - a famous factorisation algorithm for that can break some currently used encryption techniques if implemented in the quantum realm. 

Phil's accompanying blog post: https://muircheartblog.wpcomstaging.com/2026/05/26/quantum-computing-one-universe-is-more-than-enough/ 

Computerphile is supported by Jane Street
pool.editorial_reason: Clear, credible explainer of Shor's algorithm from a trusted educational channel.

pool.description: GPT-5.6 Sol is back, and I ran it through my full How I AI vibe benchmark against GPT-5.6 Terra, Luna, Claude Fable 5, and Sonnet 5 across five categories: PRDs, prototypes, wireframes, debugging, and agentic voice. Sol won by a meaningful margin on my Claire Weighted Index (70% my taste, 30% Terminal Bench 2.1), and I also tested two use cases I can't stop thinking about: building a gamified home
pool.editorial_reason: Structured benchmark comparison across models with a stated methodology, useful for builder decisions.

pool.title: This MCP Gives Claude Code 600,000+ Real Design References
pool.description:
Get 20% off your Mobbin subscription: https://mobbin.com/griffin #ad  

Claude Code can now pull from over 600,000 real UI screens before it designs anything — real onboarding flows, paywalls, settings pages, and empty states from apps that actually shipped. In this video I connect the Mobbin MCP to Claude Code and walk through exactly how to set it up, how to prompt it so it pulls the right refer
pool.editorial_reason: Concrete MCP integration demo pulling real shipped UI references into Claude Code workflows.

pool.description:
Eu testei mais de 100 skills pro Claude Code. Uma delas passou de 150 mil estrelas no GitHub, e só 5 ficaram instaladas de verdade no meu computador. Nessa aula eu mostro o que cada uma faz, o problema que resolve, e instalo tudo ao vivo, pra você sair daqui com o setup rodando também.

pool.description:
DoorDash runs Claude Code across their entire company and recently gave every one of their 4,000 employees access to Cowork.
